title: Can We Air-Condition Our Way Out of Climate Change?
gist: A deadly Western European heat wave — France's hottest day ever recorded — exposes the continent's structural lack of air conditioning (only ~5% of UK and ~3% of German homes have it vs ~90% in the US), framing AC as a near-term life-saving necessity even though it can't solve climate change.

insights.claim: Only about 5 percent of UK homes and 3 percent of German homes have cooling systems versus nearly 90 percent of US homes in 2020, leaving Europe ill-equipped for extreme heat.
insights.speaker: Bob Grant
insights.so_what: A vast structural under-penetration implies a multi-decade European AC/heat-pump installation growth runway as heat waves intensify.
insights.quote: only about 5 percent of U.K. homes and 3 percent of German homes have cooling systems
insights.locator: While nearly 90 percent of homes in the United States

insights.claim: France recorded its hottest day ever (above 111°F in parts) on June 23, 2026, and the UK registered its highest-ever June temperature (above 96°F), with red alerts, school closures, and disrupted transport across the region.
insights.speaker: Bob Grant
insights.so_what: Record-breaking European heat is a recurring, escalating risk to public health, infrastructure, and productivity.
insights.quote: France recorded its hottest day ever (with temps reaching above 111 degrees Fahrenheit in some parts of the country)
insights.locator: Yesterday (June 23), France recorded its hottest day

insights.claim: Elevated ocean temperatures are driving the jet-stream disruption and heat dome baking Europe, linking the heat wave to climate change as a symptom.
insights.speaker: Bob Grant
insights.so_what: Ocean-warming-driven jet-stream disruption suggests these European heat events recur, sustaining cooling-demand and climate-adaptation theses.
insights.quote: elevated water temperatures in nearby oceans driving the jet stream disruption and resultant heat dome that is baking the continent
insights.locator: The intense and dangerous heat is also most likely

insights.claim: Air conditioning cannot solve climate change but may be necessary to save lives during intense heat waves, the author argues directly.
insights.speaker: Bob Grant
insights.so_what: Frames AC as adaptation (life-saving) not mitigation, supporting cooling demand while leaving emissions concerns unresolved.
insights.quote: No. But in the midst of intense heat waves it may be necessary to save lives.
